---
title: "DATA3888 Volatility Modelling Report"
author: "Finance 6"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    lightbox: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
jupyter: python3
---

> Version: aligned with the latest backend and Streamlit workflow ,reads backend artifacts and report-ready figures
> Need to complete:1."To be continue".

```{python}
#| label: setup

from pathlib import Path
import pandas as pd
from IPython.display import display, Markdown, Image

ROOT = Path(".")
ARTIFACT_RUNS = ROOT / "artifacts" / "runs"
REPORT_FIGURES = ROOT / "plots" / "report_figures"
DASHBOARD_FIGURES = ROOT / "figures"


def md(text: str):
    display(Markdown(text))


def placeholder(msg: str):
    md(f"> **Placeholder:** {msg}")


def latest_run_dir():
    if not ARTIFACT_RUNS.exists():
        return None
    runs = sorted([p for p in ARTIFACT_RUNS.iterdir() if p.is_dir()], reverse=True)
    return runs[0] if runs else None


RUN_DIR = latest_run_dir()


def load_artifact(name: str) -> pd.DataFrame:
    """Load a parquet artifact from the latest backend run."""
    if RUN_DIR is None:
        placeholder("No backend run artifacts were found. Run the backend pipeline from Streamlit first.")
        return pd.DataFrame()
    path = RUN_DIR / name
    if not path.exists():
        placeholder(f"Missing artifact `{name}` in `{RUN_DIR.as_posix()}`.")
        return pd.DataFrame()
    try:
        return pd.read_parquet(path)
    except Exception as exc:
        placeholder(f"Could not read `{path.as_posix()}`: {exc}")
        return pd.DataFrame()


def show_png(path: str, width: int = 900):
    """Display one static PNG if it exists; otherwise show a clear placeholder."""
    img = ROOT / path
    if not img.exists():
        placeholder(f"Missing figure `{path}`. Need a dashboard screenshot.")
        return
    display(Image(filename=str(img), width=width))


def show_table(df: pd.DataFrame, n: int | None = None):
    if df.empty:
        placeholder("Table unavailable because the relevant backend output is missing or empty.")
        return
    display(df if n is None else df.head(n))


def artifact_status_table():
    expected = [
        "config.json",
        "status.json",
        "predictions.parquet",
        "metrics.parquet",
        "feature_importance.parquet",
        "universe_summary.parquet",
        "stock_similarity.parquet",
    ]
    rows = []
    for name in expected:
        path = RUN_DIR / name if RUN_DIR else None
        rows.append({
            "name": name,
            "path": path.as_posix() if path else "No run directory found",
            "exists": bool(path and path.exists()),
            "size_bytes": path.stat().st_size if path and path.exists() else None,
        })
    return pd.DataFrame(rows)


metrics = load_artifact("metrics.parquet")
predictions = load_artifact("predictions.parquet")
feature_importance = load_artifact("feature_importance.parquet")
universe_summary = load_artifact("universe_summary.parquet")
similarity = load_artifact("stock_similarity.parquet")

if RUN_DIR is not None:
    md(f"Using backend run artifacts from `{RUN_DIR.as_posix()}`.")
```

```{python}
#| label: generate-report-figures

import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path
from IPython.display import display, Markdown

PROJECT_ROOT = Path.cwd()
script_path = PROJECT_ROOT / "back_end" / "generate_report_figures.py"

if not script_path.exists():
    display(Markdown("> **Placeholder:** `back_end/generate_report_figures.py` was not found. Static report figures were not regenerated."))
else:
    result = subprocess.run(
        [sys.executable, str(script_path)],
        cwd=str(PROJECT_ROOT),
        capture_output=True,
        text=True,
    )
    if result.stdout:
        print(result.stdout)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        if result.stderr:
            print(result.stderr)
        display(Markdown("> **Placeholder:** Report figure generation did not complete successfully. Existing figures, if present, will still be used below."))
```


## Introduction

Traders use volatility forecasting models for many reasons, including but not limited to modelling inventory risk, portfolio optimisation and value at risk VaR analysis. Depending on the application, traders, market makers and portfolio managers require tools which have different accuracy vs latency tradeoffs. For instance, a portfolio manager calculating their 1 day VaR may want the most accurate model, while a researcher creating an options pricing algorithm for a highly liquid stock may prioritise speed at the cost of some accuracy. Hence, it is highly important for market participants to choose the model that is the best fit for their given problem.

We created a tool which lets market participants visualise and understand the fundamental tradeoffs between latency and accuracy they would be making when choosing any given volatility prediction model.

Our project aims to help traders answer the question: 

> **What is the best model given latency constraints, for a single stock or multiple stocks?**

We find these core findings to be highly relevant for market makers and quantitative researchers who must regularly make the choice between minimising latency and having the models that fit the data best. As shown above, we see clear tradeoffs between model inference latency and model loss. Our tool exposes this to traders, and lets them make informed decisions that most fit their specific strategy and the universe of assets they wish to trade.

## Problem Context and Research Question

> To be continue---research question: framed around model selection under latency constraints

## Data and Feature Engineering

Within the original dataset of 112 stocks, we cannot directly use the raw stock data for prediction. Before prediction, we must perform feature engineering to transform the raw data into variables that can be used to predict stock trends and data changes.

In addition to basic features such as market volatility magnitude, return variation, and the trend of market volatility using scrolling windows of different lengths to represent price changes over time, we added features related to time behavior, price spreads, buying and selling pressure, and market depth.

These features can uncover additional market structure information in subsequent predictions.

After feature generation, we performed basic data cleaning, removing missing values and factors with low information content. This avoids the impact of excessively small data on model predictions, reduces prediction complexity, and improves model stability.

## Modelling Workflow

Raw parquet order book data<br>
        ↓<br>
Feature engineering and feature cache<br>
        ↓<br>
Model configuration in Streamlit<br>
        ↓<br>
Backend model run<br>
        ↓<br>
Saved artifacts in artifacts/runs/&lt;run_id&gt;/<br>
        ↓<br>
Dashboard visualisation ＆ Quarto report figures

The overall modeling process can be understood as a complete workflow from raw data processing to the presentation of charts and the final data results. 

First, read the original stock order information in PRQUET format. The original data records the basic information of different stocks in the trading market and also serves as the basis for our feature extraction and subsequent work. Since the original dataset cannot be used for model training, feature engineering is needed to convert all the trading information in the order book, such as volatility changes and price trends, into variables that the model can understand. To enhance the stability of the model and reduce errors, some processed variable conditions and features will be retained and directly used in subsequent model predictions.

After completing the data preparation, users select the model, set the training parameters and decide whether to perform feature screening through the Streamlit interface. After the configuration is completed, the background will automatically complete the subsequent processes, including data processing, feature reading, cross-validation, model training, and final prediction. After each model configuration run is completed, users can obtain the predicted results of the model through the interface, including the model accuracy rate and the evaluation of model metrics. Additional charts can present data details and specific model content to users. The prediction results can be clearly presented by comparing the effects among different models. 

Finally, these experimental results will be further integrated into the visualization interface and the Quarto reporting system, presenting the model performance through charts and results to help explain the final report output.

## Forecasting Models

In the model section, we selected multiple prediction models for comparison to observe the performance differences in volatility prediction under different assumptions.

### HAR-RV

HAR-RV, as a traditional volatility prediction model, is used to establish benchmarks. It assumes that market volatility will be jointly affected by different time scales, and thus uses historical volatility information to predict future changes.

### Linear Regression

In terms of linear models, linear regression was first employed to observe whether there is a relatively simple linear relationship between artificially constructed features and future volatility.

### Ridge Regression

Ridge Regression is added to reduce the overfitting problem through regularization and improve the stability of the model under a large number of correlated variables.

### LASSO

LASSO further enhances the automatic feature screening capability, gradually weakening the variables with less impact, thereby retaining more crucial information.

### Random Forest

Random forests learn more complex interaction relationships among variables by combining multiple decision trees, while reducing the fluctuations brought by a single model.

### XGBoost

XGBoost continuously improves its prediction effect by gradually correcting errors and usually performs well for structured financial data.

By simultaneously comparing traditional models, linear models, and machine learning models, the applicability of different methods in the volatility prediction task can be evaluated more comprehensively.

## Evaluation Framework

### metrics & validation

To analyze the model performance more objectively from the data perspective, in this project, we adopt the method of repeated cross-validation to conduct experiments on the data. We use a 50% cross-validation approach and a ratio of 20% to 80% for model testing and training.

Through repeated cross-validation, the influence of other factors on the experimental results can be reduced. The evaluation of the model after repeated cross-validation will be more stable.

After the model's prediction, we added other indicators to measure the model's predictive ability. RMSE can be used to reflect the overall error size of the model, MAE is used to represent the deviation degree of prediction under average conditions, and QLIKE is a commonly used evaluation index in the study of financial volatility, mainly because it is more sensitive to the estimation error of the variance.

Through the experimental results of different metrics, it helps us observe the model stability from different perspectives, facilitating subsequent chart presentation for the final analysis and comparison of model performance.

### latency-constrained evaluation

> To be continue---

```{python}
#| label: metrics-overview

if metrics.empty:
    placeholder("Metrics artifact unavailable. Run the backend pipeline first.")
else:
    cols = [c for c in ["model", "fold", "rmse", "mae", "qlike", "mean_inference_ms", "inference_ms"] if c in metrics.columns]
    if cols:
        show_table(metrics[cols].head(15))
    else:
        show_table(metrics.head(15))
```

## Results

Our results show clear separation among different loss functions, and among different stocks.

We define a model as winning on a particular stock if that model produces the lowest overall loss for the most time id’s.

When choosing the RMSE loss function and evaluating across all stocks in the dataset, we found the XGBoost model to perform the best among all models, with a simple decision tree and multivariate linear regression performing second and third best respectively.

When choosing the QLIKE loss function, we found GJR-Garch(1,1) to significantly out-perform every other model, winning on 62 out of 112 stocks. XGBoost and regular Garch(1,1) had the second and third most wins respectively.

Importantly however, we note that both Garch models have markedly higher inference latency time, with GJR-Garch(1,1) having inference time latency of 5912 microseconds compared to XGBoost’s 0.713 microseconds.

Best model is subjective. Depends highly on the given loss function users want to optimise and your goal.

### Accuracy--Latency Trade-off

This section answers the question, **What is the best model given latency constraints, for a single stock or multiple stocks?**

A Pareto-optimal model means there is no other model that is both faster and more accurate at the same time.  
Models closer to the lower-left region are preferable because they are both faster and more accurate.

```{python}
#| label: fig-accuracy-latency

show_png("plots/report_figures/pareto_frontier.png")
```

### Model Metric Comparison

The model comparison chart includes accuracy metrics such as root mean square error(RMSE) and QLIKE.

These metrics allow for comparison of model performance from both general regression and volatility perspectives.

```{python}
#| label: fig-model-metrics

show_png("plots/report_figures/model_metric_comparison.png")
```

> To be continue---Figure.........

```{python}
#| label: model-metrics-table

if metrics.empty:
    placeholder("Model metric table unavailable.")
else:
    overall = metrics[metrics["fold"] == 0] if "fold" in metrics.columns else metrics
    keep = [c for c in ["model", "rmse", "mae", "qlike", "mape", "rmspe", "pearson_r"] if c in overall.columns]
    if keep:
        show_table(overall[keep].sort_values(keep[1]) if len(keep) > 1 else overall[keep])
    else:
        show_table(overall.head(20))
```

### Single-Stock Model Selection

The individual-stock analysis allows users to inspect how models behave for a selected stock and time window. This is important because the best model for one stock may not be the best model for all stocks.

```{python}
#| label: fig-single-stock

show_png("plots/report_figures/single_stock_actual_vs_predicted.png")
```

> To be continue---Figure.........

### Universe-Level Model Selection

The universe-level analysis compares model behaviour across multiple stocks. This helps answer whether one model performs well generally, or whether different stocks require different models.

```{python}
#| label: fig-universe-wins

show_png("plots/report_figures/universe_best_model_counts.png")
```

```{python}
#| label: universe-summary-table

if universe_summary.empty:
    placeholder("Universe summary artifact unavailable.")
else:
    keep = [c for c in ["stock_id", "mean_volatility", "rmse", "mae", "qlike", "best_model"] if c in universe_summary.columns]
    if keep:
        show_table(universe_summary[keep].head(20))
    else:
        show_table(universe_summary.head(20))
```

### Feature Importance (perhap this section should be moved forward?)

Feature importance helps explain which variables contribute most to model predictions. This supports interpretability and helps connect model behaviour back to the order book features.

```{python}
#| label: fig-feature-importance

show_png("plots/report_figures/feature_importance_top10.png")
```

## Dashboard and Visualisation Interface

> To be continue---

### Model Specification Page

```{python}
#| label: dashboard-model-spec-screenshot

show_png("figures/dashboard_model_specification.png")
```

> To be continue---

### Individual Stock Page

```{python}
#| label: dashboard-individual-screenshot

show_png("figures/dashboard_individual_stock.png")
```

> To be continue---

### Universe Page

```{python}
#| label: dashboard-universe-screenshot

show_png("figures/dashboard_universe.png")
```

> To be continue---

## Limitations(?)

> To be continue---

## Conclusion

> To be continue---(Overall, we built a useful tool for trades. Although it has some limitations, it provides genuinely useful insights and lets traders make more informed decisions than they would otherwise be able to make. )

## Appendix

```{python}
#| label: appendix-artifact-audit

md("### Backend artifact availability")
show_table(artifact_status_table())
```
